meta.purpose: Structured knowledge base for a clinical decision-support tool (differential diagnosis + treatment pathway). Not a substitute for clinical judgment or specialist referral.

input_schema.description: Suggested fields to collect from user in the tool before running matching logic
fields: systemic_symptoms (list of free text or checkboxes)

kp_distribution_reference.purpose: Quick-reference on keratic precipitate (KP) distribution pattern as a differential-diagnosis clue, supplementing IOP. Sourced from cross-referenced ophthalmology literature (Arlt's triangle concept + AAO EyeNet 'Viral Anterior Uveitis' + Ocular Immunology & Inflammation review), not from Kanski Ch.12 directly - Kanski only mentions the Arlt-triangle pattern in passing under general KP description. Included because slit-lamp exam is universally available (zero additional cost), making KP distribution a very high-value discriminative sign in resource-limited settings.

arlt_triangle.description: Classic KP distribution: inverted/base-down triangle concentrated on the CENTRAL-INFERIOR cornea, apex pointing up. Caused by gravity + aqueous convection currents (rising along warm iris, falling along cooler cornea).
arlt_triangle.clinical_meaning: Suggestive of a granulomatous inflammatory process, but NOT pathognomonic and NOT etiology-specific on its own - can occur in both non-infectious (sarcoidosis, VKH) AND infectious (TB, syphilis) granulomatous disease.
typical_entities: aau_idiopathic_hlab27 (non-granulomatous, smaller KP but same gravity-driven inferior tendency)

diffuse_beyond_midline_pattern.description: KP distributed DIFFUSELY across the ENTIRE corneal endothelium, extending beyond the horizontal midline/equator (i.e. beyond the classic inferior Arlt's-triangle confinement), typically FINE/STELLATE in morphology rather than large mutton-fat.
diffuse_beyond_midline_pattern.clinical_meaning: This is the KEY EXCEPTION to the Arlt's-triangle rule and a discriminative clue toward a VIRAL or Fuchs etiology. Not pathognomonic alone, but a strong contributory signal, especially combined with elevated IOP, sectoral/patchy iris atrophy, and (for Fuchs specifically) absence of posterior synechiae.
diffuse_beyond_midline_pattern.supporting_note: Additional literature clue (non-Kanski): fresh PIGMENTED KP (rather than the classic non-pigmented fine KP) further supports a viral cause when combined with diffuse/beyond-midline distribution.

linear_turks_line_pattern.description: KP deposited in a linear vertical formation on the inferior cornea (as opposed to the triangular Arlt pattern).
linear_turks_line_pattern.clinical_meaning: Recognized alternative distribution pattern, less specific/less commonly emphasized than Arlt's triangle or the diffuse viral pattern - low scoring weight, mainly useful to avoid mis-classifying it as 'diffuse beyond midline'.

kp_distribution_reference.combination_rule_for_scoring: KP distribution should NEVER be used as a stand-alone diagnostic feature. It should be combined with IOP pattern, iris atrophy pattern (diffuse vs sectoral vs absent), presence/absence of posterior synechiae, and granulomatous morphology (fine vs mutton-fat) - Kanski and subsequent literature both note no single KP feature is diagnostic in isolation.

diseases.name: Acute Anterior Uveitis (Idiopathic / HLA-B27-associated)
key_features: Rapid onset pain, photophobia, redness, watery discharge
Ciliary injection, miosis
Fibrinous exudate common, hypopyon common (immobile, fibrin-rich)
Small non-granulomatous KP
HLA-B27 positive in ~50% otherwise-well AAU, ~90% if associated spondyloarthropathy

kp_distribution.detail: Small non-granulomatous KP, gravity-driven inferior concentration typical of standard AAU - contrast with the diffuse/beyond-midline pattern seen in viral anterior uveitis and Fuchs.
kp_distribution.source_note: General Arlt's-triangle principle applied to non-granulomatous AAU (cross-referenced literature, not Kanski-specific)

investigations.name: Sacroiliac joint X-ray if spondyloarthropathy suspected

treatment.first_line: Topical steroid (prednisolone acetate 1% or dexamethasone 0.1%) tapered over 5-6 weeks
treatment.severe: Intracameral tPA if dense fibrin; regional steroid if non-compliant
treatment.systemic: Rarely required for isolated anterior disease

diseases.prognosis: Generally good if managed adequately; more variable if underlying systemic/ocular disorder present

diseases.name: Behçet Disease
key_features: Transient MOBILE hypopyon in a relatively WHITE eye (classic distinguishing sign)
Recurrent oral aphthous ulcers (>=3 times/12 months) + at least 2 of: genital ulceration, ocular inflammation, characteristic skin lesions, pathergy reaction
Retinal vasculitis (arteritis AND phlebitis, unlike pure venous in sarcoid)
Vitritis universal with active posterior disease
associations: Turkey/Middle East/Far East ('Silk Road') higher prevalence

treatment.immediate_indication: Behçet with retinal vasculitis = IMMEDIATE treatment, not early/delayed
treatment.first_line: Systemic steroid + azathioprine (2.5 mg/kg/day) per EULAR 2008
treatment.severe: Cyclosporine (2-5mg/kg/day) or infliximab + azathioprine + steroids for >2-line VA loss or macular/vasculitis involvement
treatment.alternative: Interferon-alfa (6 million IU/day SC) with/without steroids
treatment.avoid: Anticoagulants not recommended; azathioprine + IFN-alfa combo risk myelosuppression

diseases.prognosis: Severe visual loss up to 2/3 of males at 10 years without aggressive treatment; better with early biologics

diseases.name: Fuchs Uveitis Syndrome (Fuchs Heterochromic Iridocyclitis)
key_features: Heterochromia iridis (affected eye often hypochromic)
Diffuse STELLATE KP over entire endothelium (pathognomonic pattern)
Diffuse iris atrophy with loss of crypts
POSTERIOR SYNECHIAE ABSENT (except rarely post-cataract surgery)
Eye virtually always WHITE even during exacerbation
Posterior subcapsular cataract extremely common
Glaucoma develops in up to 60%
Vitritis with anterior gel opacities can be dense
diseases.critical_note: Do NOT treat with long-term topical steroid for chronic low-grade inflammation - not helpful in this specific entity (exception to usual CAU management)

iop_pattern.detail: Glaucoma develops in up to 60% of involved eyes; typically a LATE manifestation but occasionally present at diagnosis. Several mechanisms suspected (fine radial angle vessels/small irregular peripheral anterior synechiae seen on gonioscopy - source of Amsler sign haemorrhage on AC incision).
iop_pattern.source_note: Kanski Ch.12, Fuchs Uveitis Syndrome section

kp_distribution.detail: Diffuse STELLATE KP over the ENTIRE corneal endothelium is one of the most classic/recognizable examples of KP distribution extending beyond Arlt's triangle - this is the reference entity most textbooks use to illustrate the 'exception to Arlt's triangle' pattern.
kp_distribution.source_note: Kanski Ch.12 (diffuse stellate KP) + cross-referenced literature explicitly using Fuchs as the classic example of diffuse/beyond-midline KP (non-Kanski)

investigations.name: Usually clinical diagnosis; investigate only to exclude alternatives

treatment.approach: Long-term monitoring for glaucoma/cataract; topical steroid short-term only for moderate/severe exacerbations
treatment.cataract_surgery: Increased complication risk (poor mydriasis, hyphema, zonular dehiscence)
treatment.glaucoma: May need surgical (drainage device/trabeculectomy with mitomycin-C)

diseases.name: Uveitis in Juvenile Idiopathic Arthritis (JIA)
key_features: INVARIABLY ASYMPTOMATIC - detected by slit lamp screening, NOT by complaint
Injection usually ABSENT even with severe uveitis
PS common in longstanding undetected cases
Band keratopathy and cataract common in severe cases
Oligoarticular JIA (ANA+ 75%) has highest uveitis risk (~20%)

screening_schedule.oligoarticular_psoriatic_polyarticular_e: 2-monthly x6 months, then 3-4 monthly until age 12
screening_schedule.systemic_onset_and_RF_positive: at least initial exam, some suggest annual

treatment.adjunct: Short-acting cycloplegic (cyclopentolate) - avoid amblyopia in young children
treatment.escalation: Systemic immunosuppressive (methotrexate, infliximab, adalimumab) considered EARLY - improves visual prognosis
treatment.emerging: Tocilizumab (IL-6 blocker) shown effective in refractory JIA-uveitis

iop_pattern.detail: Glaucoma listed as a common serious complication (along with amblyopia, maculopathy, cyclitic membrane, phthisis). Not typically an early/acute diagnostic sign since JIA uveitis itself is asymptomatic.
iop_pattern.source_note: Kanski Ch.12, Uveitis in JIA section

diseases.name: Vogt-Koyanagi-Harada (VKH) Syndrome
diseases.course: phasic: prodromal -> acute uveitic -> convalescent -> chronic recurrent
key_features: Prodromal: meningitis-like symptoms, tinnitus/vertigo/deafness
Acute: bilateral granulomatous anterior + multifocal posterior uveitis, Dalen-Fuchs nodules, exudative retinal detachment
Convalescent: alopecia, poliosis, vitiligo, 'sunset glow' fundus, Sugiura sign
OCT: subretinal septae; height of subretinal fluid correlates with disease activity
diseases.diagnostic_criteria_table: Complete VKH = criteria 1-5; Incomplete = 1-3 + (4 or 5); Probable (isolated ocular) = 1-3 only

kp_distribution.detail: As a granulomatous panuveitis, VKH's anterior component classically shows mutton-fat KP with the typical gravity-driven inferior/Arlt's-triangle concentration - unlike the diffuse pattern seen in viral entities.
kp_distribution.source_note: General granulomatous-KP principle applied to VKH's granulomatous anterior uveitis component (cross-referenced literature, not Kanski-specific)

investigations.name: Lumbar puncture if uncertain (transient lymphocytic pleocytosis, melanin-laden macrophages)

treatment.first_line: High-dose oral prednisolone 1-2mg/kg/day tapered over 3-6 months, may precede with IV methylprednisolone pulse 500-1000mg/day
treatment.adjunct: Topical steroid + cycloplegic for anterior component
treatment.escalation: Immunosuppressives for steroid-resistant; infliximab EARLY if no response to steroids

diseases.urgency_note: Early treatment strongly recommended (listed among 'early treatment' indications)

diseases.name: Sympathetic Ophthalmia (SO)
diseases.course: onset 2 weeks to 3 months post-injury in 65%
key_features: History of penetrating trauma with uveal prolapse, OR multiple vitreoretinal surgeries
Dalen-Fuchs nodules (granulomas between Bruch membrane and RPE) - shared with VKH
Sympathizing eye: irritation, blurred vision, photophobia, loss of accommodation
diseases.epidemiology: Incidence ~0.2-0.5% after penetrating injury; 0.19% meta-analysis; 0.01% after intraocular surgery
diseases.prevention: Enucleation of severely injured eye within 10-14 days considered historically effective if hopeless visual prognosis

treatment.first_line: High-dose oral prednisolone, may start with IV methylprednisolone
treatment.escalation: Immunomodulatory treatment should be considered EARLY

diseases.prognosis: 75% retain VA better than 6/60 with aggressive therapy; relapse in 50% (may be delayed years) - long-term follow-up mandatory

diseases.name: Sarcoidosis (ocular)
diseases.course: AAU in acute-onset systemic disease; CAU (granulomatous) in older/chronic pulmonary disease
key_features: IWOS 7 key intraocular signs: mutton-fat KP/nodules, trabecular meshwork nodules/tent-shaped PAS, vitreous snowballs/strings of pearls, multiple chorioretinal peripheral lesions, nodular/segmental periphlebitis with candle-wax drippings, optic disc/choroidal nodules, other (conjunctival nodules etc)
Lofgren syndrome: erythema nodosum + bilateral hilar lymphadenopathy + polyarthralgia (good prognosis, more common in women)

investigations.name: Negative tuberculin skin test (paradox - supports sarcoid)

treatment.posterior: Systemic steroids +/- immunosuppressive (methotrexate, azathioprine, ciclosporin, TNF inhibitors e.g. adalimumab)
treatment.neovascularization: Scatter photocoagulation to ischemic areas on FA

iop_pattern.detail: Glaucoma listed among typical complications alongside cataract, PS, band keratopathy, vitreous haemorrhage, maculopathy.
iop_pattern.source_note: Kanski Ch.12, Sarcoidosis Investigation/Ocular features section

kp_distribution.detail: Mutton-fat KP is one of the IWOS 7 key signs of intraocular sarcoidosis; classically distributed gravity-driven inferior/Arlt's-triangle pattern, distinct from the diffuse pattern of viral anterior uveitis.
kp_distribution.source_note: Kanski Ch.12 (IWOS mutton-fat KP sign) + cross-referenced literature on Arlt's-triangle association with granulomatous disease (non-Kanski)

diseases.name: Ocular Toxoplasmosis
diseases.granulomatous: spill-over anterior uveitis may be granulomatous or resemble FUS
key_features: Single focus of fluffy white retinitis adjacent to OLD PIGMENTED SCAR = 'satellite lesion' (classic, most common presentation)
'Headlight in the fog' = white retinal nidus viewed through vitritis
Optic disc oedema common
De novo foci (no old scar) more common in immunocompromised
Most common identifiable cause of focal retinitis
Spill-over anterior uveitis common - may resemble FUS or be granulomatous; elevated IOP may follow
diseases.epidemiology: Responsible for 20-60% of posterior uveitis depending on country (85% in Brazil)

investigations.name: Toxoplasma IgG (exposure) / IgM (recent, critical in pregnancy)
PCR intraocular fluid (16-67% sensitive, highly specific)

indications_for_treatment: Sight-threatening lesion (macula, papillomacular bundle, optic disc, major vessel)
treatment.classic_triple_therapy: Pyrimethamine (loading 75-100mg 1-2 days then 25-50mg/day x4wks) + sulfadiazine 1g QID + oral folinic acid + prednisolone 1mg/kg (started after 24-48h antimicrobial)
alternatives: Co-trimoxazole (low-cost, better tolerated, maybe slightly less effective)
Clindamycin 300mg QID added or instead of pyrimethamine
Intravitreal clindamycin+dexamethasone (as effective as triple therapy in reactivated infection)
treatment.duration: 4-6 weeks treatment, complete healing over 4-6 months
treatment.prophylaxis: Co-trimoxazole 160/800mg TID x12 months reduces recurrence from 22% to 3% for up to 3 years
treatment.pregnancy: Careful selection; intravitreal therapy or azithromycin+clindamycin+/-prednisolone may be appropriate; spiramycin for prevention of transmission
treatment.avoid: Depot intra/periocular steroids (e.g. triamcinolone) - can cause uncontrolled progression

iop_pattern.detail: 'Spill-over' anterior uveitis is common in ocular toxoplasmosis. It may be granulomatous or resemble FUS. Elevated IOP may follow.
iop_pattern.source_note: Kanski Ch.12, Toxoplasmosis Ocular features section

diseases.name: Ocular Toxocariasis
diseases.laterality: unilateral in ~2/3 with permanent visual impairment
key_features: Chronic endophthalmitis: leukocoria, strabismus, floaters, unilateral visual loss
Posterior pole or peripheral granuloma WITHOUT inflammation in older child/adult - incidental finding
CRITICAL DIFFERENTIAL: must distinguish from RETINOBLASTOMA

treatment.antihelminthic: Mebendazole/thiabendazole - caution, worm death may worsen inflammation

diseases.name: Cytomegalovirus (CMV) Retinitis
diseases.course: progressive without treatment; indolent (peripheral) vs fulminant patterns
diseases.laterality: one eye initially, bilateral in 50% if untreated
key_features: Classic 'pizza pie'/'Margherita pizza' appearance: dense white retinal infiltration + flame-shaped haemorrhages along vascular arcades
Anterior uveitis usually mild
Strong association with LOW CD4+ count
Immune Recovery Uveitis (IRU) - severe inflammation post-ART reconstitution, can progress to phthisis
diseases.context: Common opportunistic infection in AIDS; also immunocompromised from other causes
diseases.screening: Low CD4 counts: 3-monthly if <50/uL, 6-monthly 50-100/uL, yearly if >100/uL

treatment.mainstay: ART (restores ability to suppress CMV) - discontinue antiviral when CD4 >100-150/uL
treatment.systemic: Valganciclovir oral - induction 900mg BID up to 3 weeks, maintenance 900mg daily (watch neutropenia, treat with filgrastim)
treatment.local: Intravitreal ganciclovir (2mg), foscarnet (1.2mg), or cidofovir (20ug) for macula/optic nerve-threatening lesions
treatment.surgical: Vitrectomy + endolaser + silicone oil for CMV-related RD (~75% success)

diseases.prognosis: Poor if macula involved despite treatment

diseases.name: Progressive Retinal Necrosis (PRN/PORN)
key_features: MINIMAL anterior uveitis and vitritis - KEY DIFFERENTIATOR from CMV retinitis and ARN
Multifocal deep yellow-white infiltrates, may involve macula early giving cherry-red spot
Predominantly in AIDS patients or other severe immunocompromise
diseases.prognosis: Extremely poor - no light perception outcome in >50% of eyes

treatment.approach: Immune rescue with ART + aggressive antiviral (IV+intravitreal ganciclovir and foscarnet)
treatment.surgery: Vitreoretinal surgery for RD often yields poor results

diseases.name: Acute Retinal Necrosis (ARN)
diseases.etiology_group: infectious (viral - HSV in younger, VZV in older patients)
key_features: PROMINENT anterior uveitis and vitritis (panuveitis) - contrast with PRN
Discrete peripheral retinal necrosis foci, well-defined borders
Circumferential spread, occlusive retinal vasculitis (arteritis)
Typically otherwise healthy individuals
diseases.diagnostic_criteria: American Uveitis Society criteria (panuveitis + peripheral necrosis foci + circumferential spread + occlusive vasculitis + rapid progression without treatment)

treatment.first_line: Aciclovir IV 15mg/kg q8h x10-14 days then oral 800mg 5x/day x6-12 weeks (reduces second-eye involvement risk)
treatment.adjunct: Intravitreal ganciclovir/foscarnet may improve prognosis; systemic steroid 24h after antiviral start
treatment.avoid: Laser retinopexy - no effect on RD rate

diseases.prognosis: More than half achieve only 6/60 due to retinal/optic nerve ischemia or RD

diseases.name: Presumed Ocular Histoplasmosis Syndrome (POHS)
key_features: Classic triad: multiple white atrophic 'histo spots' (~200um), peripapillary atrophy, ABSENCE OF VITRITIS
Macular neovascularization (MNV) late manifestation in <5%
diseases.epidemiology: Endemic areas e.g. Mississippi river valley USA

treatment.monitoring: Amsler grid weekly if macular histo spot present (25% MNV risk)

diseases.name: Tuberculosis-associated Uveitis
key_features: Choroidal granuloma/tubercle (dome-shaped lesion), can form 'tuberculoma' (abscess-like)
Serpiginoid choroiditis pattern - mimics idiopathic serpiginous choroidopathy (important DD)
Retinal vasculitis preferentially VENOUS with occlusion/ischemia
Possible link to Eales disease as hypersensitivity reaction

kp_distribution.detail: TB anterior uveitis is typically granulomatous with mutton-fat/large KP and broad PS, following the classic gravity-driven Arlt's-triangle distribution rather than the diffuse pattern of viral anterior uveitis.
kp_distribution.source_note: Cross-referenced literature on granulomatous KP distribution (non-Kanski specific for TB, but consistent with Kanski's general granulomatous-KP description)

treatment.first_line: Prolonged multi-drug anti-TB therapy (often 4 drugs initially) under specialist supervision
treatment.adjunct: Topical/systemic steroids concomitantly to reduce inflammation-induced damage, especially early weeks (retard paradoxical worsening)
treatment.monitor: Optic neuropathy if ethambutol used; rifabutin can cause anterior uveitis
treatment.note: Treating latent TB in uveitis patients with TB meds halves recurrence risk

diseases.name: Ocular Syphilis (Acquired)
diseases.course: variable; can occur at any stage
key_features: Roseolae = dilated iris capillaries -> yellowish nodules
Acute syphilitic posterior placoid chorioretinopathy (ASPPC) - large pale-yellowish subretinal lesions, more common in immunocompromised
'Ground glass' retinitis with occlusive vasculitis
Argyll Robertson pupils (CNS involvement)

kp_distribution.detail: Syphilitic anterior uveitis may be granulomatous or non-granulomatous and bilateral in 50%; when granulomatous, follows the classic Arlt's-triangle inferior distribution rather than the diffuse viral pattern.
kp_distribution.source_note: Cross-referenced literature (non-Kanski specific for syphilis KP distribution, consistent with Kanski's general granulomatous-KP description)

diseases.note: The 'great masquerader' - always test for syphilis in atypical/unexplained uveitis

investigations.name: BOTH treponemal (ELISA, sensitive/specific but positive for life) AND non-treponemal (RPR/VDRL, tracks activity) tests required
Lumbar puncture to rule out neurosyphilis

treatment.first_line: Extended course of parenteral penicillin under infectious disease specialist supervision
treatment.adjunct: Topical/systemic steroids to reduce inflammatory damage
treatment.warning: Jarisch-Herxheimer reaction - systemic response to treponemal antigen release on starting therapy, can include TRANSIENT WORSENING of ocular signs

iop_pattern.detail: Roseolae (dilated iris capillaries) may develop into yellowish nodules; IOP may be elevated.
iop_pattern.source_note: Kanski Ch.12, Acquired Syphilis Ocular features section

diseases.name: Lyme Disease (Borreliosis) - ocular
key_features: History of tick bite, erythema chronicum migrans rash
IU-associated Lyme disease often has SEVERE anterior uveitis
Facial nerve palsy in up to 25% of cases in endemic areas

investigations.name: Serology (at least 1 month post-infection; false positives occur)

treatment.early: Oral doxycycline (not in children/pregnancy), amoxicillin, or erythromycin
treatment.note: Systemic steroids should not be given without concomitant antibiotics

diseases.name: Endogenous Bacterial/Fungal Endophthalmitis
key_features: Risk factors: indwelling catheter, IV drug abuse, endocarditis, UTI, septic joint, immunosuppression
Retinal infiltrates may be an early sign reflecting hematogenous route
Mortality 5-10% - pneumonia and renal/UTI infection confer highest mortality risk
diseases.critical_note: SYSTEMIC antibiotics/antifungals are KEY (in contrast to postoperative endophthalmitis where intravitreal dominates) - search for septic focus with physician collaboration

investigations.name: Search for septic focus (endocarditis, lumbar puncture etc.)

treatment.bacterial: Systemic antibiotics per culture + intravitreal + oral fluoroquinolone; pars plana vitrectomy may improve prognosis
treatment.fungal_candida: IV amphotericin-B + oral flucytosine OR voriconazole (broad spectrum, high ocular penetration) + intravitreal 100ug/0.1ml; early pars plana vitrectomy especially in severe/unresponsive disease

diseases.name: Multiple Evanescent White Dot Syndrome (MEWDS)
key_features: White dots may DISAPPEAR before exam - imaging (FAF/OCT/ICGA) is key to diagnosis
Characteristic orange granular fovea with dulled reflex
ICGA: hypofluorescent spots often MORE numerous than clinically visible
Self-limiting, improves spontaneously by 8-10 weeks

diseases.name: Acute Posterior Multifocal Placoid Pigment Epitheliopathy (APMPPE)
key_features: Large deep yellow-white PLACOID lesions at posterior pole
Associated cerebral vasculitis possible - can cause STROKE
Poor prognostic factors: age >60, unilaterality at onset, >=6 month interval before fellow eye involved, recurrence, choroidal vein leakage
diseases.critical_note: Neurological symptoms (headache etc.) require urgent CNS imaging + lumbar puncture to rule out cerebral vasculitis

treatment.approach: Not usually required; consider steroids for macular involvement

diseases.name: Serpiginous Choroidopathy
diseases.etiology_group: non-infectious (idiopathic) - MUST exclude TB and syphilis (serpiginoid mimics)
key_features: Starts around optic disc, extends serpentine/snake-like pattern
Recurrent over years, ~25% end with VA <6/60
Associated with HLA-B7
diseases.differential: TB and syphilis can cause identical 'serpiginoid' picture - therapeutic approach very different, must rule out both

treatment.first_line: Oral or IV steroids to control activity
treatment.escalation: Immunosuppressives and infliximab, alone or combination

diseases.name: Birdshot Retinochoroidopathy
key_features: Middle-aged women, HLA-A29 positive in >95%
Cream-coloured ovoid choroidal patches radiating from disc, sparing macula
Vitritis, floaters, nyctalopia, dyschromatopsia, photopsia
MONITOR WITH VISUAL FIELD TESTING, not visual acuity
diseases.prognosis: ~1/3 end with VA <6/60, though improving with aggressive sustained IMT; choroiditis responds better to treatment than the retinal component

treatment.first_line: Sub-Tenon triamcinolone especially if monocular disease
treatment.systemic: Up to 90% need systemic treatment - early referral to uveitis specialist
treatment.agents: Steroids initially, often needs cyclosporine or mycophenolate +/- infliximab

diseases.name: Punctate Inner Choroidopathy (PIC)
diseases.laterality: young myopic women, bilateral in 80% (often asymmetric)
key_features: Minimal/absent anterior uveitis and vitritis (unlike MFC)
Small yellow-white macular spots, evolve to atrophic scars resembling POHS
MNV in up to 40% - major cause of visual loss

diseases.name: Acute Macular Neuroretinopathy (AMN)
key_features: Young healthy women, flu-like prodrome, increased incidence since COVID-19 era
Wedge-shaped parafoveal lesions best seen on INFRARED imaging
OCT: hyperreflectivity starting at outer plexiform layer
FA usually normal or faint hypofluorescence

treatment.approach: No proven treatment; self-limited but scotoma often persists

diseases.name: Herpes Simplex Anterior Uveitis
diseases.granulomatous: variable - KP may be fine/stellate/diffuse (Fuchs-like) OR large and grouped
diseases.laterality: unilateral (may occur with or without active corneal disease)
key_features: Patchy, occasionally SECTORAL iris atrophy including pigment epithelium
ELEVATED IOP common
Pupil may be larger than fellow eye
KP fine/stellate/diffuse (Fuchs-like) OR large and grouped
Often history of herpes simplex keratitis, cold sores, sometimes genital herpes
Recurrent episodes almost always involve the SAME eye

iop_pattern.detail: Elevated IOP is common in herpes simplex anterior uveitis, alongside patchy/sectoral iris atrophy.
iop_pattern.source_note: Kanski Ch.12, Herpes Simplex Anterior Uveitis section

kp_distribution.pattern: diffuse_beyond_midline (variable - can also be large/grouped rather than fine)
kp_distribution.detail: KP may be fine/stellate/diffuse across the cornea (Fuchs-like, extending beyond Arlt's triangle) OR large and grouped - variable morphology is itself part of the diagnostic ambiguity with CMV/VZV.
kp_distribution.source_note: Kanski Ch.12 (KP morphology) + cross-referenced literature on Arlt's triangle exceptions (non-Kanski)

diseases.differential_note: Distinguishing herpetic from CMV iridocyclitis can be difficult - both have elevated IOP; CMV tends to have LESS ciliary injection, LIMITED flare, FEW cells, and PS are RARE (vs herpetic which may have more prominent anterior chamber activity).

investigations.name: Clinical history (recurrent same-eye episodes, keratitis history)

treatment.first_line: Topical steroid (e.g. prednisolone acetate 1% QID) + topical cycloplegic + oral antiviral (aciclovir 400mg 5x/day)
treatment.alternative_antiviral: Famciclovir or valaciclovir - better tolerated than aciclovir
treatment.caution: Steroid may be delayed/used with caution if active epithelial disease present - add topical antiviral if so
treatment.iop_management: Treat raised IOP as necessary (topical IOP-lowering agents)

diseases.name: Varicella Zoster Virus (VZV) Anterior Uveitis
diseases.course: starts 1-3 weeks after acute skin rash (herpes zoster ophthalmicus, HZO); may be recurrent
diseases.laterality: unilateral, associated with ipsilateral HZO history
key_features: Occurs in ~50% of patients with herpes zoster ophthalmicus (HZO)
Onset 1-3 weeks after acute skin rash (occasionally without dermatitis - zoster sine herpete)
SECTORAL iris atrophy often present
Corneal sensation may be reduced
IOP RAISED
May occur in primary VZV infection (chickenpox) especially if immunocompromised

iop_pattern.detail: Sectoral iris atrophy often present, corneal sensation may be reduced and IOP raised.
iop_pattern.source_note: Kanski Ch.12, VZV Anterior Uveitis section

kp_distribution.detail: Like HSV and CMV anterior uveitis, VZV-related KP typically distribute diffusely across the entire corneal endothelium rather than being confined to the classic inferior Arlt's-triangle zone - part of the broader viral anterior uveitis triad pattern.
kp_distribution.source_note: Cross-referenced literature on Arlt's triangle exceptions (non-Kanski) - Kanski itself does not detail KP distribution specifically for VZV

treatment.first_line: Topical steroid + mydriatic, in addition to standard systemic antiviral treatment of shingles
treatment.monitoring: All HZO patients must be monitored by ophthalmologist depending on severity - up to weekly for at least 6 weeks to detect occult inflammation, then possibly long-term for late complications
treatment.recurrence: Persistence/recurrence may respond to week-long course of aciclovir 800mg 5x/day; long-term prophylaxis considered for repeated recurrence
treatment.prevention: VZV vaccination offers protection against shingles

diseases.name: Cytomegalovirus (CMV) Anterior Uveitis
key_features: More common in IMMUNOCOMPETENT individuals than previously realized (though still less prevalent than HSV/VZV-related inflammation)
ELEVATED IOP very common - can present as Posner-Schlossman syndrome (glaucomatocyclitic crisis)
Little or NO ciliary injection
LIMITED flare, FEW cells (in contrast to more florid herpetic inflammation)
Corneal endotheliitis
KP of variable morphology, sectoral iris atrophy
Posterior synechiae are RARE
Key diagnostic indicator (in some cases only): FAILURE TO RESPOND to aciclovir and/or steroids

iop_pattern.pattern: elevated - most prominent/consistent feature among the three viral anterior uveitides
iop_pattern.detail: Elevated IOP is very common. CMV has been reported as a cause of Posner-Schlossman syndrome. This, combined with minimal ciliary injection/flare/cells and RARE posterior synechiae, helps distinguish CMV from more inflamed HSV/VZV anterior uveitis.
iop_pattern.source_note: Kanski Ch.12, Cytomegalovirus Anterior Uveitis section

kp_distribution.detail: KP of variable morphology distributed diffusely (not confined to Arlt's triangle), often described as small/medium and sometimes coin-shaped endotheliitis-related lesions in the literature - contributes to the low-injection, low-flare, diffuse-KP picture typical of CMV anterior uveitis.
kp_distribution.source_note: Kanski Ch.12 (KP of a range of morphology) + cross-referenced literature on Arlt's triangle exceptions (non-Kanski)

diseases.differential_note: vs HSV/VZV anterior uveitis: CMV has LESS injection, LESS flare, FEWER cells, PS RARE, and characteristically FAILS TO RESPOND to aciclovir/steroids - this non-response can itself be the diagnostic clue.

investigations.name: PCR and antibody assay of aqueous sample
investigations.note: should be considered if clinical suspicion

investigations.name: Trial of aciclovir/steroid response (diagnostic by failure to respond)

treatment.first_line: Oral valganciclovir in proven infection - sometimes requires long-term continuation
treatment.note: IOP elevation may be persistent despite antiviral treatment

diseases.name: Lens-Induced (Phacogenic) Uveitis
key_features: Immune response to lens proteins following incomplete cataract extraction, trauma, or rarely capsular degeneration in mature cataract
Most common modern scenario: RETAINED LENS FRAGMENTS after phacoemulsification (posterior segment after capsular rupture/zonular dehiscence, or overlooked fragment in AC)
Corneal oedema common adjacent to AC lens fragment
IOP FREQUENTLY ELEVATED
Lens fragments may be visible in anterior or posterior segment
Vitritis if fragments lie within vitreous cavity

iop_pattern.detail: IOP is frequently elevated in lens-induced uveitis, often with corneal oedema adjacent to an AC lens fragment.

diseases.differential_note: Must be differentiated from bacterial endophthalmitis, which is usually more severe.

treatment.definitive: Surgical removal of all lens material from AC or via pars plana vitrectomy as required
treatment.conservative_option: Small lens fragments in posterior segment can often be managed conservatively - absorb slowly over months
treatment.adjunct: Cycloplegia and IOP-lowering treatment commonly indicated
treatment.complications: CMO, glaucoma, epiretinal membrane, rarely retinal detachment/cyclitic membrane

quick_pattern_matchers.pattern: KP diffuse across ENTIRE cornea, extending beyond Arlt's-triangle/midline + fine/stellate morphology + no posterior synechiae + eye white
quick_pattern_matchers.note: Fuchs is the classic textbook example of KP distribution violating the Arlt's-triangle rule. Combine with heterochromia + absent PS to strengthen.

quick_pattern_matchers.pattern: KP diffuse across ENTIRE cornea, extending beyond Arlt's-triangle/midline + elevated IOP + sectoral/patchy iris atrophy (any of HSV/VZV/CMV pattern present)
quick_pattern_matchers.note: Diffuse/beyond-midline KP distribution is a group-level clue for VIRAL anterior uveitis as a whole (vs granulomatous non-viral). Use IOP intensity, injection level, flare/cell count, PS presence, and antiviral/steroid response to differentiate among the three - see the disease-specific quick_pattern_matchers above/below for that finer discrimination.
quick_pattern_matchers.action: Do NOT treat this pattern alone as conclusive - always combine with at least one more discriminating feature (IOP behaviour, injection degree, PS presence/absence, treatment response) before ranking a specific virus.

quick_pattern_matchers.pattern: KP concentrated in classic inferior triangle (Arlt's triangle), mutton-fat/large, granulomatous
quick_pattern_matchers.note: Arlt's-triangle distribution is suggestive of granulomatous inflammation but is NOT etiology-specific by itself - it does not distinguish infectious (TB/syphilis) from non-infectious (sarcoid/VKH) granulomatous disease. Serology/CXR/IGRA still required to differentiate within this group.
quick_pattern_matchers.action: Never use Arlt's-triangle presence alone to rule OUT infectious causes (TB, syphilis) - always screen serology/IGRA regardless of a 'typical non-infectious-looking' granulomatous KP pattern.

quick_pattern_matchers.pattern: anterior uveitis + elevated IOP + minimal ciliary injection + limited flare/few cells + poor response to steroid/aciclovir
quick_pattern_matchers.note: Consider Posner-Schlossman syndrome. Contrast with herpes_simplex_anterior_uveitis/vzv_anterior_uveitis which also raise IOP but usually with more prominent inflammation and a positive antiviral history/response.

quick_pattern_matchers.pattern: unilateral recurrent anterior uveitis, SAME eye each time + elevated IOP + sectoral/patchy iris atrophy + past herpetic keratitis or cold sores
anterior uveitis 1-3 weeks after ipsilateral shingles rash + elevated IOP + sectoral iris atrophy + reduced corneal sensation
recent cataract surgery/trauma + elevated IOP + visible lens fragment + corneal oedema adjacent to fragment
elevated IOP noted, but patient currently ON topical/regional steroid therapy
quick_pattern_matchers.action: Consider IATROGENIC steroid-response IOP elevation before attributing to disease-specific mechanism - does not reliably discriminate between entities in this state

quick_pattern_matchers.pattern: hypopyon + mobile + white eye
hypopyon + immobile + red painful eye
heterochromia + stellate KP + no PS + white eye
child + asymptomatic uveitis + arthritis
satellite lesion adjacent to old chorioretinal scar
HIV/immunocompromised + pizza-pie retinitis
immunocompromised + rapid necrosis + minimal inflammation
healthy adult + necrotizing retinitis + prominent panuveitis
leukocoria or strabismus in child with retinal granuloma
tinnitus/vertigo + bilateral granulomatous panuveitis + exudative RD
prior penetrating trauma + fellow eye now inflamed
serpiginous/geographic choroiditis pattern
quick_pattern_matchers.note: MUST rule out TB and syphilis before labeling idiopathic serpiginous choroidopathy

quick_pattern_matchers.pattern: young myopic woman + MEWDS-like but recurrent/overlapping
wide field placoid posterior lesions + neuro symptoms
anterior uveitis + elevated IOP + minimal injection/flare/cells + PS rare + no response to aciclovir/steroid
quick_pattern_matchers.note: Non-response to treatment can itself be the diagnostic clue for CMV anterior uveitis

quick_pattern_matchers.pattern: anterior uveitis + elevated IOP + sectoral iris atrophy + history of ipsilateral HZO rash 1-3 weeks prior
anterior uveitis + elevated IOP + patchy/sectoral iris atrophy + recurrent SAME eye + history of HSV keratitis/cold sores
anterior uveitis + elevated IOP + recent cataract surgery + visible lens fragment
chronic unilateral anterior uveitis + heterochromia + stellate KP + NO PS + eye white + glaucoma present/developing
quick_pattern_matchers.note: Glaucoma in FUS develops in up to 60% of eyes, typically a LATE manifestation

iop_reference_table.purpose: Quick-reference summary of IOP behaviour across uveitis entities in Kanski Ch.12 - useful as a scoring signal since tonometry is universally available even in resource-limited settings (unlike HLA typing, PCR, etc.)
iop_reference_table.general_principle: Kanski notes IOP in anterior uveitis 'may be reduced as the result of impairment of aqueous secretion by the ciliary epithelium, or elevated due to a variety of mechanisms, including the use of topical steroids.' So IOP direction alone is not pathognomonic, but PATTERN + CONTEXT is discriminative.

entities_with_IOP_ELEVATION_as_a_notable.strength: strong - very common, often with minimal other inflammatory signs; a hallmark feature
moderate - common finding alongside sectoral/patchy iris atrophy
moderate - common finding alongside sectoral iris atrophy, reduced corneal sensation
moderate-strong - frequently elevated, associated with corneal oedema near lens fragment
mentioned - IOP may be elevated with roseolae/secondary syphilis anterior uveitis
mentioned - elevated IOP may follow spill-over anterior uveitis
strong but CHRONIC/LATE - glaucoma develops in up to 60% of eyes over time, not necessarily an early/acute sign
complication-level - glaucoma is a common serious COMPLICATION of longstanding disease, not an early diagnostic clue
complication-level - glaucoma listed among typical complications
complication-level - glaucoma listed among ocular complications (with macular neovascularization, subretinal fibrosis, vitreous haemorrhage, cataract)
complication-level, END-STAGE only - glaucoma appears among end-stage disease complications (with optic/retinal atrophy, PS, cataract); NOT a feature of acute Behcet presentation

iop_reference_table.iatrogenic_caution: Topical/regional/intraocular steroid treatment itself commonly causes elevated IOP ('steroid responders') - this is a TREATMENT SIDE EFFECT, not a diagnostic clue, and must be distinguished from disease-related IOP elevation, especially in patients already on steroid therapy for their uveitis. Regional steroid injection carries up to 25% risk of refractory IOP elevation.
iop_reference_table.clinical_utility_note: Because tonometry is essentially universally available (unlike HLA typing, PCR, ICGA), 'elevated IOP present/absent' is a HIGH-VALUE, LOW-COST checkbox to include prominently in the clinical input form - it meaningfully narrows the viral anterior uveitis differential (HSV/VZV/CMV) even when advanced investigations are not accessible.
